core_intuitions: The modern decoder block is the 2017 Transformer with a specific set of swaps, and each swap targets one of three goals — training stability, compute/memory efficiency, or final quality — at a specific location in the dataflow.

Pre-norm keeps the residual highway clean so gradients flow unblocked, whereas post-norm divides the residual through a normalization op; RMSNorm is LayerNorm minus mean-subtraction (scale-only, one reduction pass).

SwiGLU works because it splits the MLP into a content projection and a learned soft gate that multiplicatively suppresses or amplifies each content dimension — an interaction a plain ReLU/GELU MLP lacks.

Softmax mass must sum to 1, so when nothing is relevant the leftover probability piles onto the always-present first tokens (the attention sink) — which is why StreamingLLM must keep those BOS tokens in the KV cache.

When q.k dot products grow unbounded with scale/training the softmax saturates to one-hot (attention collapse); QK-norm bounds the dot product and logit soft-capping (c.tanh(z/c)) squashes extremes without hard clipping.
GQA trades away KV heads for a proportional cut in KV-cache memory, which is what makes long-context inference affordable.

new_figures.name: modarch-recipe-gpt2-vs-modern
new_figures.concept: Side-by-side annotated decoder block showing every GPT-2 choice and its modern replacement, tagged by why (stability / efficiency / quality).
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's spine — its own framing calls itself an 'annotated map of decisions.' No other chapter draws the GPT-2-vs-modern recipe as a whole; the individual mechanisms (GQA, RoPE, pre/post-norm) are owned elsewhere, but the synthesis that makes THIS chapter cohere exists only here. A single map lets the reader hold all nine choices and their locations at once.
new_figures.placement: The Modern Transformer Recipe (anchor after the recipe table intro paragraph)
new_figures.brief:
Two parallel vertical decoder-block columns with a change-gutter between them. LEFT column titled 'GPT-2 (2019)', RIGHT column titled 'Modern (2024-26)'. Each column is a residual-stream spine (a vertical line running bottom-to-top with '+' junctions) feeding two stacked sublayers: an Attention block and an MLP block, each with a Norm attached. Show these concrete differences, one row-band per difference, aligned horizontally so left and right sit at the same height:
(1) NORM PLACEMENT & TYPE: left shows 'LayerNorm' AFTER the sublayer add (post-norm: x -> Sublayer -> +x -> Norm); right shows 'RMSNorm' BEFORE the sublayer (pre-norm: Norm -> Sublayer -> +x), and draw the right residual spine as a clean unbroken line while the left spine passes THROUGH the norm box (to make 'clean highway vs divided residual' visible).
(2) POSITION ENCODING: left shows a 'learned absolute pos-embed' box ADDED at the input (bottom); right shows 'RoPE' as a small rotation glyph applied to Q and K INSIDE the attention block only.
(3) ATTENTION VARIANT: left 'MHA' drawn as N query heads each paired with its own K,V box; right 'GQA' drawn as N query heads but only a couple of shared K,V boxes (groups of query heads pointing to one KV box).
(4) MLP: left 'GELU MLP' = two weight matrices (up, down) with a GELU between; right 'SwiGLU' = three matrices with a gate-times-up merge glyph.
(5) BIAS: left linear boxes annotated '+b'; right annotated 'no bias'.
(6) EMBEDDINGS: left 'tied' (input embed and LM head share one matrix, drawn as a linked pair); right 'untied' (two separate matrices).
In the CENTER gutter, between each aligned row-band draw a short arrow from the GPT-2 choice to the modern one, each tagged with ONE small category marker from a 3-item legend: 'stability', 'efficiency', 'quality' (use distinct neutral glyph shapes — e.g. shield / gauge / star — NOT hardcoded colors; the accent class may color the modern column only). Keep every label to 1-3 words. Use a wide viewBox so both full columns and the gutter fit without overlap; ensure the two column titles and the legend do not collide. Reading order: bottom (input/embeddings) to top (output/LM head).

new_figures.name: modarch-attention-sink-mass
new_figures.concept: Why softmax mass conservation forces a probability 'dump' onto BOS, and why StreamingLLM must keep sink tokens in the KV cache.
new_figures.rationale: The attention-sink intuition is genuinely owned by this chapter and drawn nowhere else. It is counterintuitive (a content-irrelevant token getting the most attention) and the mechanism — 'the mass has to go somewhere' — is exactly the kind of idea a single picture makes click. It also directly explains the practical StreamingLLM eviction rule.
new_figures.placement: Attention Sinks and Sink Tokens > The 'attention sink' phenomenon
new_figures.brief:
Three stacked mini-panels sharing a horizontal token strip [BOS, t1, t2, t3, ..., tN] with one query arrow above.
PANEL A 'relevant context present': attention shown as vertical bars over the tokens, with a few mid-sequence tokens carrying tall bars (the relevant ones) and BOS carrying a small bar. Beside it a small fixed-height bucket labeled 'sum = 1' shown mostly filled by the relevant tokens.
PANEL B 'nothing relevant': the relevant bars collapse to near-zero; because the bucket must still sum to 1, the leftover mass visibly flows (draw a curved flow arrow) onto BOS, which now has a tall bar labeled 'attention sink — probability dumped here'. Make the visual point that BOS grew ONLY because the others shrank and the total is conserved.
PANEL C 'StreamingLLM': a sliding attention window drawn over the strip covering the most recent tokens, with the first few tokens PINNED (a small pin/anchor glyph) and kept even though they are outside the window; middle tokens greyed/evicted. A short caption: 'keep the sinks; evict the middle — dropping BOS collapses attention.'
Use the accent class only for the sink bar in Panel B; all other bars muted. Keep the three panels clearly separated with small titles; ensure the flow arrow in Panel B does not overlap the bucket or labels. Everything must read as a static frame.

new_figures.name: modarch-logit-taming
new_figures.concept: How unbounded attention logits collapse the softmax, and how QK-norm and logit soft-capping each keep it from saturating.
new_figures.rationale: QK-norm and logit soft-capping are two distinct guard rails the chapter introduces together; the underlying failure mode (softmax saturation / attention collapse from exploding logits) is a shared core intuition served by neither the numerics chapter nor the attention chapters. One figure that shows the problem and both fixes side by side is more foundational than either fix alone.
new_figures.placement: QK-Norm: Stabilizing Attention Logits at Scale > The problem: logit explosion
new_figures.brief:
Three linked panels left-to-right under a shared banner 'keeping the softmax from saturating'.
PANEL 1 'the problem': show a q vector and k vector with a large dot-product value feeding a softmax; the resulting attention distribution is a bar chart that is essentially one-hot (one bar near 1, the rest near 0) labeled 'attention collapse'. Add a small caption 'logits grow with scale / long training'.
PANEL 2 'QK-norm': show q and k each passed through an 'RMSNorm' box that renders them as unit-length arrows on a small unit circle before the dot product; annotate that the dot product is now bounded to O(d_k). The resulting softmax bar chart now spreads across several tokens (healthy distribution).
PANEL 3 'soft-cap': plot the squashing curve z -> c*tanh(z/c) on small axes, with the identity line y=x drawn faintly for reference; show the curve tracking the identity near the origin (linear region) and flattening to horizontal asymptotes at +c and -c. Label the cap value as 'c' (illustrative, e.g. a small tick), mark 'linear near 0' and 'asymptote at +/-c'. Add a one-line takeaway strip beneath all three: 'QK-norm bounds the input; soft-cap squashes the extreme — both prevent one-hot collapse.'
Use the accent class for the healthy/spread distribution and the soft-cap curve; muted for the collapsed distribution and reference line. Invent no numbers beyond a tiny illustrative cap tick. Ensure axis labels and the unit circle do not overlap adjacent panels.

new_figures.name: modarch-swiglu-gating
new_figures.concept: SwiGLU as a soft gate modulating a content projection element-wise, versus a plain single-path MLP.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's key claim is that SwiGLU adds a 'multiplicative interaction the MLP lacked before' — a gate that suppresses or amplifies each content dimension. Activation-curve and full-block figures live in the transformer-block chapter, but the gate-times-content DATAFLOW is not drawn anywhere, and it is the mechanistic heart of why SwiGLU beats GELU at equal parameters.
new_figures.placement: SwiGLU: A Better MLP Activation > SwiGLU mechanics
new_figures.brief: Left inset (small, greyed) 'plain MLP': x -> W1 -> GELU -> W2, a single path, labeled 'one path, no interaction'. Main diagram 'SwiGLU': input x fans out into TWO parallel linear projections drawn as boxes — top path 'gate = xW' passing through a 'SiLU' box that renders it as a soft 0..1-ish gate (draw a small SiLU curve glyph inside), bottom path 'up = xV' the raw content. The two paths meet at an element-wise-multiply node (circle with a dot/times symbol) producing 'gate (x) up', then a final 'down = W2' projects back to model width. Draw the multiply as per-dimension: show a short column of a few content cells being scaled up or down by aligned gate cells (one gate cell tall -> amplify, one near-zero -> suppress), to make 'the gate dials each dimension' literal. Add a small note that the intermediate width shrinks to 8d/3 so the three matrices stay iso-parameter with a 4x two-matrix MLP. Use the accent class for the gate path; muted for content and the plain-MLP inset. Keep labels to the given short symbols (xW, SiLU, xV, down). Ensure the per-dimension multiply illustration is legible and does not crowd the down-projection.

summary: This survey chapter currently has zero figures. Rather than duplicate mechanism figures already owned by neighboring chapters (GQA, RoPE, pre/post-norm, activation curves, KV-cache growth all exist elsewhere), the plan proposes four chapter-owned figures: (1) a signature GPT-2-vs-modern annotated recipe map that gives the whole chapter a spine, (2) the attention-sink mass-conservation intuition, (3) a combined QK-norm + logit soft-cap 'taming the softmax' figure, and (4) a SwiGLU gate-times-content dataflow. All are static — no multi-step process here genuinely needs motion. Discipline was applied by excluding RMSNorm/LayerNorm and pre/post-norm figures (served by tblock- figures in ch06) and GQA/RoPE (served in ch04/ch05).
